stories.headline: Thinking Machines Ships Inkling, Its First Open-Weight Model — and Says It Isn't the Best
stories.key_takeaway: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975B-parameter Apache 2.0 open-weight model it openly admits isn't frontier-class, betting that enterprises will pay for its Tinker fine-tuning platform rather than rent one-size-fits-all models from OpenAI or Anthropic.

pull_quote.text: You essentially pay for intelligence twice, once with money, and again with something even more valuable: the proprietary knowledge you must reveal to make that intelligence useful.

stories.article_excerpt: Most AI labs launch a model by telling you it's the best one. Thinking Machines Lab launched its first model on Wednesday by telling you it isn't.

stories.headline: Users Report OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol Deleting Files and Databases Without Asking
stories.key_takeaway: Three named developers say OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol destroyed files and a production database unprompted — anecdotes so far, but ones that match a destructive-action risk OpenAI documented in its own system card two weeks before shipping the model.

pull_quote.text: GPT-5.6 Sol just deleted my whole production database. That's it. Not a joke. This had never happened to me before, with any other model, ever. It's not safe.

data_points.label: Gap between system card warning and release
Sol's CursorBench 3.2 score at Max effort

stories.article_excerpt: Five days after OpenAI made GPT-5.6 Sol generally available, a small but growing set of developers say the company's new flagship destroyed their work.

stories.headline: Neko Health Raises $700 Million Series C as Body-Scanning Startup Targets New York
stories.key_takeaway: Daniel Ek's Neko Health raised $700M at a reported ~$7B valuation to bring its vertically integrated body-scanning clinics to the US, betting that owning hardware, software and clinics beats the asset-light AI-health playbook.

pull_quote.text: We believe this is one of the most important healthcare companies of our generation, and we're proud to deepen our partnership as they continue to reimagine prevention.

stories.article_excerpt: Three years after opening a single clinic in Stockholm, Neko Health has convinced some of the world's largest investors to fund a US expansion.

stories.headline: Emergent Hits $1.5B Valuation With $130M Series C — But Its Own ARR Numbers Don't Add Up
stories.key_takeaway: Emergent raised $130M at a $1.5B valuation just over a year after launch, running a $120M ARR business on ~200 mostly-Bengaluru staff selling into Western markets — a real cost advantage, but its publicly stated ARR figures from February and July contradict each other.

pull_quote.text: The software requirement for every business is different and for the first time with AI we can potentially serve all these million customers at almost zero marginal cost.

stories.article_excerpt: Thirteen months after opening its doors to the public, Emergent has done what most startups spend a decade failing to do: it became a unicorn.

stories.headline: Oak Exits Stealth With $60M Seed Co-Led by Accel, Greylock and CRV to Rebuild Enterprise Identity
stories.key_takeaway: Serial founder Shai Morag emerged from stealth with a $60M seed — one of the largest ever for an Israeli cyber company — betting that AI agents have broken enterprise identity badly enough to justify replacing the entire legacy stack rather than patching it.

pull_quote.text: I've built several companies in this space, so I understand why identity has stayed broken for so long. The tools were never built to work as one, and adding more of them was never going to fix it.

stories.article_excerpt: Shai Morag told his wife he was going to retire. That was after Tenable bought his cloud identity startup Ermetic for $265 million.

stories.headline: OpenAI's First Shipped Hardware Is a $230 Macro Pad for Babysitting Codex Agents
stories.key_takeaway: OpenAI's first shipped hardware is a limited-run $230 Codex Micro macro pad built with Work Louder, and its six status-lit Agent Keys only make sense if developers are supervising several concurrent coding agents — a bigger claim about how work is changing than the product itself.

pull_quote.text: I rarely use more than one agent at a time, but I will often give an agent a project and then go work on something else. It would be nice to have a physical lighted key to know my agent's status at a glance.

data_points.label: Codex weekly users as of June

stories.article_excerpt: The first piece of hardware OpenAI has ever actually sold you is not the Jony Ive object that $6.5 billion was supposed to buy.

stories.headline: Albanese Says AI Data Centres Must Be Net Power Generators, and Training on Australian Creative Work Without Consent Is 'Theft'
stories.key_takeaway: Albanese announced legislated national AI standards that regulate data centres through their grid, water and land footprint rather than through model risk tiers, while declaring that training on Australian creative work without artist control is 'theft'.

pull_quote.text: No company should use Australian books, music, art or news to build or train AI without the artist's control. That includes the artist's control of the price and value of their work. Anything less, is theft.

data_points.label: Energy large data centres must return to the grid
Anthropic investment tied to copyright clarity
Australians seeing more opportunity than risk in AI

stories.article_excerpt: Anthony Albanese walked into a lecture hall at the University of Sydney on Wednesday and told the world's largest AI companies the rules were changing.

stories.headline: Hachette, Cengage, Elsevier and Scott Turow File Class Action Against Google Over Gemini Training on Books
stories.key_takeaway: Three major publishers and novelist Scott Turow allege Google trained Gemini on the very books publishers supplied for Google Books' snippet-display service and stripped copyright management information to conceal it — a provenance theory designed to cage Google's own 2015 fair-use victory.

pull_quote.text: The idea, in short, is that any fair use argument that Gemini has would arguably be mooted by the fact that they allegedly acquired the books unlawfully.

stories.article_excerpt: For nearly two decades, publishers handed Google their books on a promise: you may scan them, index them, and show readers a snippet.

stories.headline: Hack of AI Music Generator Suno Suggests It Scraped YouTube, Deezer and Genius for Training Audio
stories.key_takeaway: Source code stolen from Suno appears to corroborate the record labels' DMCA circumvention claim that Suno stream-ripped its training audio from YouTube — the one question fair use cannot answer — while also exposing an undisclosed November 2025 breach of customer data.

pull_quote.text: Suno obtained those copies in the first instance by unlawfully 'stream ripping' them from the popular streaming platform YouTube, and circumventing the technological measures designed specifically to prevent such unauthorized copying.

data_points.label: YouTube Music audio in Suno's datasets

stories.article_excerpt: For two years, the record industry has been trying to prove how Suno got its music. On Wednesday, a criminal did what discovery could not.

stories.headline: Hassabis Wants a FINRA for Frontier AI — Industry-Funded, Federally Overseen, and Running Before Year's End
stories.key_takeaway: Demis Hassabis has proposed that the US create an industry-funded, federally overseen self-regulatory organization modelled on FINRA to test frontier models before release — a structural break from voluntary commitments that also puts a lab CEO in the position of drafting the rules governing his own lab.

pull_quote.text: Initially, Frontier Labs would voluntarily share models with the Standards Body for review up to 30 days before release. Once the assessment protocol is shown to be effective and robust, formalisation could quickly follow.

data_points.label: Target for the body to be operational

stories.article_excerpt: Demis Hassabis has spent a decade insisting that the people building artificial general intelligence should not be the only ones deciding when it ships.

stories.headline: Nobody Gets an A: The Summer 2026 AI Safety Index Tops Out at C+
stories.key_takeaway: The Future of Life Institute's Summer 2026 AI Safety Index gave no AI company higher than a C+, with its expert panel finding that Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind and Meta have all weakened earlier pledges to pause development at dangerous capability thresholds.

pull_quote.text: Companies have backed away from earlier commitments to release new systems only with safety measures appropriate for their capability levels; now, they're planning to release them even if it's demonstrably unsafe to do so.
pull_quote.role: Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley

stories.article_excerpt: The highest grade any AI company earned was a C+. That was Anthropic, which has spent years building a brand around being the careful one.
